title: Bayesian Volatility Modeling
text:
This project applies Bayesian inference to estimate and compare two volatility models on S&P 500 (IVV ETF) weekly data. The models were trained on 2013–2017 data and evaluated on 2018–present, capturing events like the COVID-19 market crash.


GARCH(1,1) models conditional variance as a deterministic function of past squared shocks and past variance:
$$_t^2 = _0 + 1 a{t-1}^2 + 1 {t-1}^2$$
Stochastic Volatility (SV) treats log-volatility as a latent AR(1) process, allowing for smoother, continuous changes:
$$y_t = t (), h{t+1} = + (h_t - ) + _t $$



Instead of finding a single “best” parameter set via maximum likelihood, Bayesian inference computes the full posterior distribution of parameters using MCMC sampling (Stan). This gives more robust estimates and naturally quantifies uncertainty.








The SV model achieved lower MSE on both training and test data overall
GARCH(1,1) better captured the COVID-19 volatility spike
SV performed better during stable periods
Both models benefit from Bayesian estimation





Python 3.9+, Shiny for Python, Plotly, pandas, NumPy
Stan (PyStan) for Bayesian MCMC sampling
ArviZ for Bayesian diagnostics

title: Home
text:
Welcome to my Data Science portfolio. I’m a final-year BSc Data Science and Economics student at Central European University, with a study abroad year at the London School of Economics. My interests lie in quantitative finance, macro investing, and Bayesian statistics.
Previously at Goldman Sachs (Equities Market Risk) and Raiffeisen Bank International. Incoming Macro Investment Associate at a hedge fund in Abu Dhabi.


1. Bayesian Volatility Modeling — Comparing GARCH(1,1) and Stochastic Volatility models for predicting S&P 500 weekly volatility using Bayesian inference with Stan.
View Project →
2. Portfolio Optimization — Comparing five quantitative portfolio construction strategies on a diversified 16-asset portfolio, backtested over 2021–2024.
View Project →

title: About
text:
I’m a Data Science and Economics student at Central European University, graduating in June 2026. I spent my third year at the London School of Economics studying Stochastic and Actuarial Methods in Finance, Macroeconomics, and Valuation & Hedging — earning a Distinction.
My interests sit at the intersection of quantitative finance, macroeconomics, and data science. I’ve worked at Goldman Sachs (Equities Market Risk) and Raiffeisen Bank International, and I’m joining a macro-focused hedge fund in Abu Dhabi as an Investment Associate after graduation.



Programming: Python, SQL, Stata
Statistics & ML: Bayesian inference, time series analysis, volatility modeling, econometrics, machine learning

title: Projects
text:
A selection of data science projects showcasing statistical modeling, Bayesian inference, optimization, and interactive visualization.


An interactive application comparing two Bayesian volatility models on S&P 500 weekly data (2013–2023). Built with Shiny for Python, featuring real-time model comparison and Plotly visualizations.
Highlights:

Bayesian parameter estimation using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (Stan)
GARCH(1,1) vs Stochastic Volatility model comparison
Out-of-sample prediction including the COVID-19 volatility spike
Object-oriented Python design with abstract classes, inheritance, and polymorphism

View Project Details →




Comparing five portfolio optimization strategies — Efficient Frontier, Max Sharpe, Global Minimum Variance, Risk Parity, and Equal Weights — on a diversified 16-asset portfolio backtested over 2021–2024.
Highlights:

Mean-Variance Optimization with efficient frontier construction
Maximum Sharpe Ratio, GMV, and Risk Parity strategies
Backtesting against S&P 500 and equal-weight benchmarks
Interactive strategy comparison with Sharpe ratios and asset allocation views

View Project Details →

title: Portfolio Optimization
text:
This project compares five portfolio optimization strategies on a diversified 16-asset portfolio spanning U.S. equities, international equities, real estate, commodities, and fixed income. Models were trained on 2014–2021 data and backtested on 2021–2024.


Mean-Variance Optimization (Efficient Frontier) — Minimizes portfolio volatility for a given target return. Three points along the frontier were selected: conservative, moderate, and aggressive.
Maximum Sharpe Ratio — Maximizes risk-adjusted return by optimizing the ratio of excess return to volatility.
Global Minimum Variance — Minimizes total portfolio risk without requiring expected return estimates.
Risk Parity — Equalizes the risk contribution of each asset to the overall portfolio.
Equal Weights (Benchmark) — Naive 1/N allocation across all assets.



16 assets across 5 classes: U.S. Equities (SPY, AAPL, MSFT, JNJ, JPM, BA, NKE, AMZN), Non-U.S. Equities (IEMG, ILF, DB1.DE), Real Estate (IYR, REM), Commodities (GLD, CL=F), and Fixed Income (ISTB).








EF_3 (aggressive) achieved 38.0% total return, beating SPY (32.9%)
Max Sharpe and EF_2 performed comparably to SPY
GMV underperformed (-1.1%) — minimizing risk alone was insufficient
Equal Weights outperformed most optimization strategies





Python 3.9+, SciPy (SQP optimization), pandas, NumPy, Plotly
Data: Yahoo Finance, U.S. Treasury rates (FRED)
